studies.title: Experiences of frontline healthcare workers during COVID-19
key_findings: Moral distress from rationing care and PPE shortages
Institutional communication perceived as inadequate and delayed
Peer bonding strengthened under shared adversity

quotes.text: We had to decide who gets the ventilator. That stays with you.
Management sent emails. We needed them on the floor.

studies.title: Global nursing workforce and COVID-19
key_findings: Feeling abandoned by institutions while being publicly celebrated
International differences in PPE access created moral anger
Finding purpose through collective action and advocacy

quotes.text: They called us heroes but wouldn't give us masks.
I stayed because my colleagues needed me, not because the hospital asked.

studies.title: Healthcare workers' mental health during COVID-19
key_findings: Fear of infecting family members dominated daily life
Spiritual coping mechanisms (prayer, faith) provided resilience

quotes.text: I slept in my car for two months so I wouldn't bring it home.
My faith kept me going when medicine couldn't help.

studies.title: Healthcare workers experiences during COVID-19
key_findings: Meaning-making through narratives of purpose and duty
Post-peak psychological fallout worse than during peak

quotes.text: I'm a dermatologist. They put me on a COVID ward. I felt useless.
The worst part came after. When it slowed down, everything hit me.

cerqual_findings.finding_text: Healthcare workers experienced profound moral distress from resource rationing, triage decisions, and witnessing preventable suffering.
A pervasive sense of institutional abandonment -- being publicly praised while privately unsupported -- was a dominant source of anger.
Peer solidarity and collective identity among frontline staff emerged as the primary coping mechanism, valued above institutional or psychological support.
